characters.blurb: Unified Macedon and Greece’s leash at Chaeronea. Murdered in 336; Alexander inherited his war on Persia—and his tangled court.

characters.relation: Physician; friend who cured him in Cilicia
characters.blurb: Trusted Acarnanian doctor who risked the potion while Parmenio warned against him—Alexander drank and recovered.

characters.relation: Historian cited on the Amazon visit
characters.blurb: One of the writers Plutarch lists who dismiss the Amazon tale as fiction, alongside Philip the Chalcidian.

characters.relation: Historian cited on the Amazon visit
characters.blurb: Paired with Philip of Theangela among authorities who call the Amazon episode a fiction.
After the Hydaspes, Alexander left Porus his kingdom and gave another friend—this Philip—a far larger neighboring government.
Claimed divine dreams about her son’s birth. Her feuds with Philip and later with Antipater darken Alexander’s rear.
Lost at Issus and Gaugamela, fled, and was murdered by Bessus. Alexander buried him royally and took his household.
Philip’s old marshal, useful and cautious. After Philotas’s fall Alexander had him killed before news could fly to Ecbatana.
Commander of the Companions, arrogant in prosperity. Tortured and killed when a plot touched his silence.
Boyhood companion and chiliarch. His death at Ecbatana broke Alexander into extravagant mourning and divine honors.
Taught the young prince Homer and philosophy. Later cooled when Alexander killed Callisthenes and ruled as a god.
Aristotle’s kinsman who recorded the march, opposed proskynesis, and died in custody after the pages’ conspiracy.

characters.relation: Saved him, then slain at dinner
characters.blurb: Black Clitus cut down the Persian at the Granicus who would have killed Alexander—then died by Alexander’s spear in a drunken quarrel.
Held Europe while Alexander conquered. Olympias’s enemy; his summons to Asia and his son Cassander haunt the Life’s end.
Blunt Macedonian favorite for traditional ways. Alexander joked Hephaestion loved Alexander, Craterus the king.
Cretan friend who sailed from the Indus to the Persian Gulf and told Alexander the sea tales he craved.
Cynic-trained writer who steered the fleet and embroidered the Indian wonders Plutarch sometimes doubts.
Telmessian soothsayer who read entrails and birds for the king from the Granicus to Babylon’s last days.

characters.relation: Indian king fought at the Hydaspes
characters.blurb: Tall opponent who lost elephants and army yet kept his kingdom by asking to be treated “like a king.”
The stallion only Alexander could tame; died in India and gave his name to a city.
Marriage that enraged Olympias and insulted Alexander at the wedding feast—prelude to Philip’s murder.
Prayed at the wedding for a “legitimate” heir; Alexander flung a cup, fled, and later had him killed in Asia.
Asked only that Alexander stand out of his sun—the meeting Plutarch keeps as a mirror to royal vanity.
Trusted after Hephaestion’s death with the ring in some tales; already a leading Companion in the Indian years.
Boyhood friend left with the treasure; absconded to Athens with stolen wealth when Alexander’s return neared.
Urged divine honors and mocked Callisthenes; the court foil to freer speech.
Ascetic who followed the march and burned himself alive when illness came—astonishing the Macedonians.
Antsman kinsman of Olympias who trained the boy hard and scolded him for lavish incense—until Asia’s wealth repaid the jest.
Called himself Phoenix to Alexander’s Achilles; later a Successor, here still the schoolroom courtier.
Kept the Iliad (Aristotle’s recension) under his pillow with a dagger—Achilles always the pattern.
Captured beauty Alexander married in the northeast; mother of his posthumous son in the wider tradition.
Royal Persian bride in the Susa mass marriages—union of conqueror and Achaemenid house.
Persian or Greek-Persian noblewoman remembered among Alexander’s loves before the eastern queens.
Killed his king and took the upright tiara; Alexander hunted him down and punished the regicide.
Rhodian who nearly undid the invasion in Asia Minor; his death eased Alexander’s first campaigns.
Companion whose memoirs (via others) feed the tradition; already trusted in India and at the festivals.
Named among the rising officers of the later years—another Successor latent in the Life.
Philip’s son by Philinna, impaired in mind; Olympias’s cruelty toward rivals frames his shadowed claim.
Bodyguard who murdered Philip in the theater—motive tangled in court outrage Alexander’s enemies later exploited.
Wept for Greece’s wars when he saw Alexander in Persia’s halls; old guest-friend of Philip’s court.
Hecatomnid ruler Alexander adopted as mother in Caria—local politics with a personal face.

characters.relation: Roman pair in the Parallel Lives
characters.blurb: Julius Caesar is his match: world-conquerors whose courage and fortune Plutarch weighs side by side.

characters.relation: Subject; conqueror of the Persian Empire
characters.blurb: Son of Philip and Olympias who crossed into Asia at twenty-two and never came back. He destroyed the Persian Empire, carried Greek culture to the edges of India, and died at Babylon before he was thirty-three—leaving the world divided among his generals and the question of his greatness permanently open.

characters.relation: Heroic model he carried to Asia
characters.blurb: Alexander kept the Iliad (in Aristotle's recension) under his pillow with a dagger, treating Achilles as his ancestor through Olympias and his ideal of glorious, brief life.
Philip's Argead house claimed Heraclid descent; Alexander invoked Hercules as a competing model alongside Achilles—the laborious civilizer against the glorious warrior.

characters.relation: God of Delphi and the Philippus oracle
characters.blurb: The Pythian oracle at Delphi saluted Alexander as son of the god before the campaign—one of the divine endorsements he collected to frame his conquest as heaven-blessed.
Through the Aeacid–Dionysiac lineage Olympias claimed, Alexander was kin to Dionysus, whose Indian conquest he measured himself against and competed to surpass.

characters.relation: Oracle god at Siwa; possible divine father
characters.blurb: At the oracle of Zeus Ammon in the Libyan desert the priest addressed Alexander as son of the god—an answer he neither confirmed nor denied, and which colored every later claim to divinity.
Tutor Lysimachus cast himself as Phoenix to Alexander's Achilles—a schoolroom fiction that still carried weight when the two travelled together on campaign.

characters.relation: Bodyguard who shielded him among the Malli
characters.blurb: Covered the wounded Alexander with the sacred shield from Ilion when the king leaped alone into the Mallian town; his courage saved Alexander's life in the most reckless moment of the whole campaign.

characters.relation: Antipater's son; object of Alexander's hatred
characters.blurb: Laughed at Persians doing proskynesis—and received a near-fatal grip on his hair from Alexander in return. Plutarch records his hatred of Alexander's memory and the poisoning rumor that followed.
Wrote memoirs of the expedition that Plutarch cites alongside Ptolemy—more sympathetic to Alexander than Callisthenes, less candid than Cleitarchus.
Royal chamberlain who recorded the dinner-table stories and ceremonies of Alexander's court—the source behind many of Plutarch's intimate scenes and drinking anecdotes.
Man from Chalaestra whose plot against Alexander first surfaced through a lover rather than through proper military channels—the initial link in the chain that destroyed Philotas and Parmenio.

characters.relation: Macedonian exile who fought for Darius
characters.blurb: Fled Philip's Macedon to the Persian court and served Darius, appearing among the opponents at Issus—one of the Greeks who chose the wrong side of history.

characters.relation: Carian dynast in the marriage dispute
characters.blurb: Sought a Macedonian bride for his daughter; Philip planned Arrhidaeus for the match, but Alexander secretly intervened—a rash act that revealed his fear of being supplanted.

characters.relation: Actor sent by Alexander to Pixodarus
characters.blurb: Tragic actor Alexander dispatched secretly to Caria to offer himself instead of Arrhidaeus—the blunder that led Philip to exile Alexander's closest friends.

characters.relation: Indian sage who refused Alexander's summons
characters.blurb: Senior gymnosophist who told Alexander's envoy that he needed nothing the king could give and feared nothing he could take away—the encounter Plutarch uses to test Alexander's own claim to philosophy.
The only artist Alexander permitted to carve his likeness in bronze; Lysippus alone, Plutarch says, caught the tilt of the head and the liquid glance of the eyes.
The greatest painter of the age and the only one Alexander commissioned for his official image—paired with Lysippus as the two artists who captured the king's likeness for posterity.

characters.relation: Persian who struck him at the Granicus
characters.blurb: Delivered a sword blow that sheared Alexander's helmet at the Granicus ford—saved from death only when Clitus cut down Rhoesaces from behind.

characters.relation: Persian satrap who nearly killed him at the Granicus
characters.blurb: Raised his sword to cut down Alexander from behind after Rhoesaces's blow; Clitus severed his sword-arm in time—one of the closest moments of the whole campaign.

characters.relation: Agent who offered Alexander beautiful slaves
characters.blurb: Wrote from the coast asking whether the king wished to buy beautiful boys—an offer Alexander found offensive and answered with sharp reproof for imputing such desires to him.
Sent the king silver-studded boots and other costly presents from Asia; Alexander's rebuke of him and Philoxenus illustrates his early restraint in luxury before the later Eastern habits took hold.

characters.relation: Elderly Persian satrap; father of Barsine
characters.blurb: Old Persian nobleman who had lived at Philip's court as an exile; his long familiarity with Macedonians made him and his daughter Barsine figures Alexander treated with unusual warmth.

characters.relation: Amazon queen in the disputed tale
characters.blurb: Said to have come with three hundred warrior women to meet Alexander and conceive a child by him; Plutarch lists the many sources who deny the story ever happened.
Larissaean companion at whose party Alexander drank heavily—the night from which he never recovered. Plutarch weighs the feast against the poisoning rumor.

characters.relation: Actor who performed at Alexander's court
characters.blurb: One of the two most celebrated tragic actors of the age; performed at the contests Alexander staged in Asia.

characters.relation: Eunuch who fled and betrayed Darius's family
characters.blurb: Among the women of Darius's household; escaped the camp on horseback and carried word to Darius.
Persian eunuch who won a dancing prize and was kissed by Alexander before the whole army—one of Plutarch's glimpses of the Eastern court.

characters.relation: Bactrian chief on an impregnable rock
characters.blurb: Held a sheer-sided rock Alexander chose to besiege; surrendered on terms and supplied Alexander with supplies.
Alexander found Cyrus's tomb violated, punished the desecration, and measured his own conquests against the Persian founder's—the throne of Cyrus was a recurring symbolic prize.
Lines from Euripides were quoted by Alexander and those around him on several occasions—Medea and Andromache especially—reflecting the literary culture of his Macedonian court.
Diviner Alexander summoned at Babylon after Apollodorus had sacrificed; he reported the liver defective—an omen Alexander took gravely in his final days.
Cited by Plutarch for the tradition that Olympias told Alexander the secret of his divine birth as he set out on campaign—one of several sources Plutarch weighs.

characters.relation: Indian king who submitted peacefully at Taxila
characters.blurb: Wise ruler whose dominions rivaled Egypt in size. He told Alexander the only wars worth fighting are over necessities; all else should yield to gifts. He later helped persuade Calanus to follow the march.

characters.relation: Royal page who led the conspiracy against Alexander
characters.blurb: Young Macedonian page whose plot was exposed and laid partly at Callisthenes's door. He had asked what act would most distinguish a man and received an answer that steered him toward murder. His accomplices were stoned; Callisthenes shared the disgrace.

characters.relation: Antipater's son; cupbearer; suspected of poisoning Alexander
characters.blurb: Chief cupbearer at Babylon during Alexander's final days, and son of Antipater. Six years after the death, Olympias scattered his ashes as if he had administered the fatal dose—an accusation Plutarch examines but leaves unproven.

characters.relation: Bodyguard who consoled Darius's captive women
characters.blurb: Sent after Issus to reassure the captured women of Darius that the king still lived and they had nothing to fear from Alexander. Later reproved for the extravagance of using camels to transport Egyptian wrestling-powder on campaign.

characters.relation: Indian elder who outwitted Alexander's hostage demand
characters.blurb: Eldest of an Indian delegation given a cushion by Alexander. When pressed for a hundred of the best citizens as hostages, he replied he would govern more easily by sending the worst—an answer Alexander greeted with laughter and friendship.

characters.relation: Future Indian ruler who met the young Alexander
characters.blurb: Not long after Alexander's retreat from the Hyphasis, Androcottus (Chandragupta) came to rule those very lands and gave five hundred war-elephants to Seleucus—proof, Plutarch notes, that the reports of India's vast elephant armies were no exaggeration.

characters.relation: Philip's envoy sent to the Delphi oracle
characters.blurb: Man of Megalopolis dispatched by Philip after the vision of a serpent lying with Olympias. The god commanded sacrifice and special honor to Ammon—and warned Philip he would lose the eye that had peered through a crack in the door.

characters.relation: Visionary architect consulted for Hephaestion's monument
characters.blurb: Favored for sheer boldness of design. He had once proposed shaping Mount Athos into a colossal man—left hand holding a city of ten thousand, right hand pouring a river into the sea. Alexander declined then; grief-mad after Hephaestion's death, he sought him for something even grander.

characters.relation: Bactrian chieftain; counselor in the northeastern satrapies
characters.blurb: When Alexander's soldiers despaired before Sisimithres's impregnable rock, Oxyartes assured him the defender was the greatest coward alive. Alexander took the place shortly after—a lesson that resolution could reduce what geography made invulnerable.

names: Oxyartes, one of the sons of Abuletes
characters.relation: Son of satrap Abuletes; killed by Alexander returning from India
characters.blurb: Killed by Alexander's own spear as the king purged commanders who had abused their provinces in his absence. His father Abuletes compounded the failure by bringing coined money instead of proper provisions—the talents were thrown to the horses, who ignored them.

characters.relation: Satrap who substituted coins for provisions
characters.blurb: Governor who, when Alexander's army needed food on the Indian return, brought three thousand coined talents instead. Alexander had the money thrown to the horses; they would not touch it. 'What good will this provision do us?' Abuletes was sent to prison; his son was speared.

characters.relation: Arrhidaeus's obscure mother; victim of Olympias's jealousy
characters.blurb: Woman of undistinguished birth who bore Philip's son Arrhidaeus. Olympias reportedly gave the boy drugs that ruined both his health and his intellect, leaving him the pawn Perdiccas used as a figurehead after Alexander's death.

characters.relation: Olympias's brother; consented to Philip's marriage
characters.blurb: Molossian king and Olympias's brother, who gave his consent to Philip's marriage after their meeting at Samothrace. He is the only person named in Plutarch's opening sentence as the human intermediary in the union that produced Alexander.

characters.relation: Theban lyric poet whose descendants were spared at the sack
characters.blurb: When Alexander razed Thebes he sold thirty thousand into slavery—but exempted the family of Pindar along with those who had opposed the war. One of the very few gestures of mercy in that destruction.

characters.relation: Athenian orator who mocked Alexander as a boy
characters.blurb: Foremost opponent of Macedon in Athens. He called Alexander a child when the prince was in Illyria and a youth when he was in Thessaly. Alexander resolved to appear before the walls of Athens as a man—a threat made credible by the fall of Thebes.

characters.relation: Companion dispatched to the oracle of Serapis at Babylon
characters.blurb: Sent with Seleucus to ask the god of Serapis whether Alexander should be moved to the temple during his final illness. The oracle replied he should not be moved. He was not—and died where he lay.

characters.relation: Historian who linked Alexander's birth to Diana's fire
characters.blurb: Writer from Magnesia who declared the temple of Artemis at Ephesus burned on the night of Alexander's birth because the goddess was away assisting at it—a conceit Plutarch dismisses as cold enough to have extinguished the conflagration itself.

characters.relation: Music theorist whose Memoirs record Alexander's fragrant body
characters.blurb: Aristotle's pupil and writer of Memoirs, cited for his report that Alexander's skin exhaled a remarkable sweet odor—attributed by Theophrastus to the heat of his choleric constitution.

characters.relation: Philosopher and botanist who explained Alexander's fragrance
characters.blurb: Aristotle's successor, invoked to explain why Alexander's body was said to smell of spices: the sweetest odors come from the driest, hottest climates, where the sun exhausts surface moisture before it can putrefy—a by-product of his fiery temperament.

characters.relation: Aeacid hero; Alexander's ancestor on Olympias's side
characters.blurb: Son of Achilles through whom Olympias's Molossian line claimed Aeacid descent. The lineage made Achilles not only Alexander's model but his blood forebear—a claim he honored by laying a wreath on Achilles's tomb at Troy.

Doctor attending Hephaestion at Ecbatana who had gone to the theater when his patient—fevered but impatient with the prescribed diet—ate a whole fowl and drank a large draught of wine. Hephaestion died shortly after; Alexander had Glaucus crucified.

characters.relation: Later Successor king; witness to the poisoning rumor
characters.blurb: Officer who became one of the great rival kings after Alexander's death. Plutarch records that Hagnothemis heard Antigonus speak of the ice-cold Nonacrian water supposedly used to poison Alexander—a rumor Plutarch takes seriously enough to weigh.

characters.relation: Companion who blocked Callisthenes from the royal kiss
characters.blurb: At the proskynesis banquet, he warned Alexander that Callisthenes alone had failed to make the required adoration and should not receive the royal kiss. The intervention deepened the philosopher's fall and, through Callisthenes, cooled Alexander's regard for Aristotle.

characters.relation: Theban matron who killed her attacker at the sack
characters.blurb: When a Thracian officer raped her and demanded hidden treasure, she led him to a garden well and pushed him in, then stoned him to death. She told Alexander her brother Theagenes had fallen at Chaeronea fighting his father Philip. Alexander freed her and her children at once.

characters.relation: Greek tragedian quoted in the Life
characters.blurb: Athenian tragedian whose plays Aristotle sent Alexander on campaign alongside those of Euripides and Aeschylus. Plutarch also quotes him on the need for both 'the bridle and the rudder'—a metaphor for the comprehensive training Aristotle required of the prince.

nameResolutions.note: Friend appointed to a larger government after Porus

locations.blurb: Philip’s realm at the edge of the Greek world. From here Alexander marched east—and never ruled it in person again.
Birthplace and court of his youth, where Philip’s power and Olympias’s ambitions first shaped him.
Ancestral capital where Philip was murdered in the theater—Alexander’s reign begins in blood.
Grove school where Aristotle taught Homer and politics to Alexander and the Companions’ sons.
Where the young Alexander broke the Sacred Band (338 BC). Greece’s leash passed to Macedon.
Rebelled after Philip’s death; Alexander stormed it and left a ruin as a lesson to Greece.
Where the Greeks named him captain against Persia—and where he met Diogenes asking only for light.
Spoke against Macedon, then flattered the conqueror. Alexander treated it carefully after Thebes.
Plutarch’s own shrine-town. Alexander pressed the Pythia for an omen before Asia.
First Asian stop of myth: he honored Achilles’ tomb and took armor said to be Trojan.
Strait Xerxes bridged the other way. Alexander sacrificed and stepped onto Persian soil.
River fight against satraps and Greek mercenaries—Clitus later saved him here in memory.
Key of western Asia Minor; its surrender opened the Persian road inland.
Coastal stronghold taken early—part of stripping Persia of its Aegean fleet bases.
Hard siege against Memnon’s defense; Ada of Caria later became his adoptive mother nearby.
Where he adopted Queen Ada—local rule folded into his march with a personal face.
Phrygian town of the famous knot—cut or loosed, the oracle promised Asia’s rule.
Narrow land of Tarsus and fever; Darius’s army waited beyond its passes.
Pinned between sea and hills, Darius fled and left his family in Alexander’s hands.
Seven-month siege: a causeway to the island walls—and a hard lesson in naval stubbornness.
Harbor cities that fed Persia’s fleet; taking them opened the road to Egypt.
Desert fortress that resisted after Tyre; Alexander was wounded taking it.
Persia’s rich province greeted him as pharaoh-to-be; here he founded Alexandria and sought Ammon.
Laid out on the Nile mouth—his most lasting foundation, though he never saw it flourish.
Desert trek to the oasis god who greeted him as son—fuel for later divine honors.
Treasure and captives of Darius’s court fell here while the king fled inland.
Crossed at Thapsacus toward the heartland—Gaugamela waited beyond the Tigris.
Open plain where Darius’s massed army broke; Arbela names the region in older telling.
Opened its gates after Gaugamela. Years later he returned to die there amid omens.
Achaemenid seat of gold—and later the Susa weddings joining Macedonians to Persian brides.
Where Hephaestion died and where Parmenio was killed when Philotas fell.
Boundary of the upper satrapies; crossings mark the chase into Sogdiana.
Monsoon river fight of elephants and courage; Porus kept his kingdom “like a king.”
Land of Porus, gymnosophists, and the army’s refusal to go farther—turnback at the Hyphasis.

locations.relation: Desert march that starved the army
locations.blurb: Coastal waste on the return—thirst and sand that cost more men than many battles.
Where the survivors feasted after the desert—and Nearchus rejoined with Ocean tales.
Northern country after Darius’s death—woods, the Caspian edge, and restless pursuit.
He chose Alexandria’s site by the Nile mouth—harbor, island, and fresh water together.
